name: Voilà (Turn Jupyter Notebooks into Web Apps)

auth.notes: No built-in auth; deploy behind reverse proxy with auth (nginx, JupyterHub)

performance.notes: Notebook execution at startup; kernel per user; memory grows with concurrent users

agent_readiness.notes: Converts Jupyter notebooks to standalone web apps. voila my_notebook.ipynb --port 8866. Hides code cells, shows outputs and widgets. ipywidgets interactive controls. Templates for custom styling: --template=gridstack. JupyterHub integration. voila --no-browser for headless. Custom template: voila --template=custom_template. Tornado-based server. Each user gets isolated kernel. Progressive rendering: cells execute top-down and stream results. Pre-execution: voila --prelaunch-hook=script.py.

gotchas: Each user gets a separate kernel — memory scales with concurrent users
No built-in authentication — must deploy behind JupyterHub or reverse proxy
Notebook re-executes entirely on each page load (can be slow for heavy computations)
Limited layout control compared to Streamlit or Dash
ipywidgets required for interactivity — not all plotting libraries work out of box
best_when: Converting existing Jupyter notebooks to shareable web apps without rewriting
Internal data science tools where notebook-based development is the workflow
JupyterHub deployments needing user-facing dashboard interface
When ipywidgets interactivity is already in the notebook
avoid_when: Complex multi-page applications (use Streamlit or Dash)
When full control over layout and styling is needed
summary: Voilà converts Jupyter notebooks into standalone web apps. `voila notebook.ipynb` serves the notebook as a web app, hiding code cells and showing outputs/widgets. ipywidgets provides interactivity. Tornado-based with per-user kernel isolation. JupyterHub integration. BSD-3. Best for quickly sharing existing notebooks as dashboards without rewriting. For new interactive apps, prefer Streamlit, Marimo, or Panel.
